title: Statement on the President's Decision Ordering the Divestment of Interests in Certain Assets of EMCORE Corporation by HieFo Corporation
summary: President Trump ordered HieFo Corporation, a Delaware corporation and foreign person, to divest all interests in EMCORE Corporation's digital chips and semiconductor manufacturing business after CFIUS identified national security risks related to potential access to intellectual property and diversion of indium phosphide chip supply. HieFo did not file the transaction with CFIUS until after the Committee's non-notified team investigated the transaction.
key_points: CFIUS identified national security risk from HieFo's acquisition of EMCORE's digital chips business due to potential access to intellectual property, proprietary know-how, and expertise
CFIUS identified risk of potential diversion of indium phosphide chip supply away from the United States
HieFo did not voluntarily file the transaction with CFIUS; the non-notified team investigated before HieFo filed
CFIUS's non-notified function has been enhanced by authorities from FIRRMA and ongoing appropriations
President's order directs HieFo to divest all interests and rights in the EMCORE Digital Chips Business
CFIUS reviews each transaction on a case-by-case basis considering specific facts and circumstances
why_it_matters: This represents a CFIUS enforcement action forcing a foreign person to divest interests in a U.S. semiconductor business on national security grounds. While it signals continued U.S. scrutiny of foreign investment in sensitive technology sectors, it is a single-case disposition and explicitly does not indicate a general position on countries or industries involved.
macro_context: This is a single CFIUS enforcement action regarding a specific foreign investment in semiconductor technology. The source explicitly states this case-by-case disposition is not indicative of a general position on transaction parties, countries, or industries. This does not constitute a macro catalyst as it does not involve monetary policy, economic data releases, or broad market-moving events.
